entities.description: An anonymous Middle English twelve-line tail-rhyme romance, probably composed around 1400 in the Northeast Midlands. It survives in two substantially different later-fifteenth-century witnesses and combines chivalric, penitential, hagiographical, and Breton-lay features.
entities.label: Sir Gowther
notes: Neither consulted witness nor work-level source names an author.
Northeast-Midland localization is a work-level scholarly inference and is not conflated with the c.1480 copying context of witness A.

bibliographic.citation: Karl Breul, ed., Sir Gowther: Eine englische Romanze aus dem XV. Jahrhundert kritisch herausgegeben nebst einer litterarhistorischen Untersuchung über ihre Quelle sowie den gesamten ihr verwandten Sagen- und Legenden-Kreis mit Zugrundelegung der Sage von Robert dem Teufel (Oppeln: Eugen Franck's Buchhandlung [Georg Maske], 1886).
bibliographic.title: Sir Gowther: Eine englische Romanze aus dem XV. Jahrhundert

entities.description: Breul's expanded monograph and eclectic critical edition. Its critical text is based on A, fills A's lacunae from B, occasionally adopts corrections, collates B throughout, and prints manuscript foliation, Breul's own lineation, Utterson's independent lineation, and a foot-of-page apparatus.
entities.label: Karl Breul, Sir Gowther (1886)
notes: The encoded portion comprises the title page and section VI, Text, printed pp.135-165.
Breul's preface says the critical text first appeared in his 1883 Berlin dissertation and that the 1886 text incorporates reviewed suggestions and selected changes.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1886, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff. The exact BSB item also applies No Copyright - Non-Commercial Use Only 1.0, whose premise is that the underlying work is public domain while contract terms may govern the digital representation.
rights.component: 1886 printed edition and medieval text as the underlying work of the BSB digital object
rights.jurisdiction: United States and provider rights-statement premise

entities.description: A paper miscellany copied about 1480 in a North-Midland context. Sir Gowther begins acephalously on current f.11r, where the upper outer corner and opening text are damaged, and ends with its explicit on f.27v. The following work begins on f.28r.

holding.notes: Acquired for the national collections in 1925.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.3.1 (Breul A)

physicalDescription.extent: xii + 216 + xviii folios; Sir Gowther at current ff.11r-27v
notes: Full red morocco binding dated 1964.
Breul's historical discussion gives A fol.11a-28a, while the current NLS catalog, DIMEV v1.0, and direct digital images place the work at ff.11r-27v.
The Sir Gowther text is associated with Richard Heeg's hand; the codex also contains material by James Hawghton and additions in other hands.

rights.basis: The anonymous text is embodied in a manuscript copied about 1480 and predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical-access and image-reuse terms are separate.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Sir Gowther text embodied in Adv.MS.19.3.1; physical ownership and digital images excluded

British Library, Royal MS 17 B XLIII (Breul B)

subject.description: Both witness edition references and Bibliography.xml Breul1886a

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact carrying CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00115.xml; the abstract medieval work, source edition, encoding, witnesses, and facsimiles are separate entities.
Breul's own witness key identifies A as Advocates' Library MS 19.3.1 (current NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1) and B as Royal MS 17 B XLIII. No direct witness-to-witness derivation is asserted.
The U-M encoding selects only the 1886 title page and section VI, Text (printed pp.135-165), not the rest of Breul's XVI + 241-page study.

Breul says A contains 738 written lines and B 691; his critical text has 756 positions. DIMEV v1.0 reports A as 757 lines. The XML has 766 L elements only because a ten-line B ending is quoted inside the apparatus note at critical line 754.
All 2,262 HI elements are italic. Breul explains that italics mark expanded abbreviations, B-supplied passages, and occasional editorial interventions; they are not manuscript emphasis.

The 221 numeric MILESTONE elements serialize Breul's left-margin critical numbering and Utterson's independent right-margin numbering, so duplicates and non-monotonic values must not be treated as one line sequence. Additional folio milestones retain historical and occasional encoding anomalies.
The XML encodes stanza 43 as N='63' with heading LXIII. Direct inspection of printed p.155, BSB canvas 179, confirms the source heading is XLIII.

The NLS manifest's canvas identifiers for 37 and 70 return HTTP 404. Work-portion boundaries therefore use tested 200-response IIIF Image API URLs while retaining canvas numbers, current folios, and ZIP members as locators.

The NLS cache is complete relative to all 482 provider-published canvases, not to every physical leaf: the provider explicitly states that numerous modern blank flyleaves at the beginning were not all captured.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00115, an encoding of the title page and section VI, Text, from Karl Breul's 1886 Sir Gowther monograph. Breul's 756-position eclectic critical text is based chiefly on manuscript A, National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1, current ff.11r-27v; he fills A's lacunae and records variant readings from manuscript B, British Library Royal MS 17 B XLIII, ff.116r-131v. A complete BSB digital copy of the 1886 volume and the complete 482-canvas NLS digital object are cached with fixity and exact work boundaries. No complete public BL facsimile was found in the official routes checked on 2026-07-12; the official catalog, physical-manuscript request, reference enquiry, and digitisation-order routes are recorded separately.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably around 1400; surviving witnesses are later fifteenth-century copies; source edition 1886
catalogSummary.region: Probably the Northeast Midlands of England; witness A was copied in a North-Midland context
catalogSummary.title: Sir Gowther

catalogingSubject.label: Sir Gowther
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse romance Sir Gowther, not either surviving manuscript as a physical object, Karl Breul's 1886 German monograph as a whole, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. The represented text is Breul's eclectic 756-position critical text, based chiefly on witness A (NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1) but supplied and corrected from witness B (BL Royal MS 17 B XLIII); its apparatus and source title page are retained as separate content layers.

contentParts.label: Breul source title page

locators.value: first DIV1, four P elements; PB REF='2'

contentParts.summary: The four-paragraph transcription of the 1886 edition's long German title, editor statement, place, publisher, and date.

contentParts.label: VI. Text: Sir Gowther

locators.value: second DIV1, 63 main LG and 756 L elements outside NOTE descendants

notes: Breul supplies critical lines 1-12, 42-44, and 463-465 from B where A lacks text; other B readings and editorial interventions are recorded in the apparatus.
The XML's stanza 43 has LG/@N='63' and HEAD 'LXIII'; direct inspection of printed p.155, BSB canvas 179, confirms the source reads 'XLIII'.

Breul prints his line numbers in the left margin, Utterson's independent numbering in the right, and manuscript folio milestones; the 221 numeric MILESTONE elements therefore do not form one canonical line sequence.
contentParts.summary: Breul's eclectic Middle English critical text, chiefly based on witness A and supplied or corrected from B, with an A-version explicit after line 756.

contentParts.label: Breul's inline critical apparatus

locators.value: 642 NOTE elements; one nested LG with ten L elements in the note attached to critical line 754

notes: The 1,163 italic HI elements inside notes and 1,099 elsewhere preserve Breul's editorial typography; they are not manuscript emphasis.
contentParts.summary: Six hundred forty-two encoded footnotes, chiefly in German, recording A and B readings, lacunae, emendations, foliation, and editorial explanation; the note at critical line 754 quotes B's wholly divergent ten-line ending and its explicit.

assertion.notes: This dates the work, not either later manuscript witness.
Both known witnesses are later-fifteenth-century copies; neither is treated as the composition event.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 sections: a four-paragraph source title page and VI. Text. The latter contains 63 main LG elements, 756 main-stream L elements, 63 stanza HEAD elements, 642 NOTE elements, and one further LG with ten B-ending L elements nested in the note at critical line 754. Across the file are 32 PB, 286 MILESTONE, two TRAILER, one CLOSER, and 2,262 italic HI elements. Thus 766 is the raw encoded L total, while 756 is the critical poem extent and ten belong to an apparatus quotation.

formStatements.description: A tail-rhyme narrative represented by Breul in sixty-three twelve-line stanzas, rhyme pattern aabccbddbeeb.

assertion.notes: The text calls its story a lai of Britain and DIMEV indexes it as a Breton lai; METS notes that the work also crosses hagiographical and romance categories.
Witness B's explicit 'vita sancti' also records a medieval hagiographical framing without proving a single exclusive genre.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German prose occurs in Breul's modern apparatus but is not treated as an intellectual language of the medieval work.
TEXT/@LANG='enm ger' describes the encoded source layers; only Middle English is cataloged as the language of the medieval work.
The XML proofing note dated 9-6-16 is a repository encoding history marker, not a work, witness, or edition date.

Current NLS foliation and visual boundaries take precedence for the digital work portion; Breul's historical A. fol. milestones and occasional XML milestone anomalies are retained as source features, not silently normalized.

next_steps: Consult later work-specific source studies and dialect research beyond the directly inspected reference editions.
Keep analogues, textual sources, and physical exemplars distinct; do not convert Breul's organizing comparison into a proven direct exemplar.
openQuestions.question: What exact date, place, and source relations best explain Sir Gowther, Robert le Diable, and the wider demonic-paternity and penitential traditions?

next_steps: Collate complete high-resolution images of both witnesses against a modern diplomatic edition.
Treat Breul's view that neither surviving copy directly derives from the other, and that B is a refined revision, as editorial analysis rather than a confirmed physical genealogy.
openQuestions.question: What exact stemma or sequence of redaction connects the substantially different A and B versions?

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's count with a diplomatic transcription of current ff.11r-27v.
Consult NLS foliation history to determine whether Breul's f.28a reflects an older numbering rather than another physical leaf.
Preserve the 756 critical positions, 738 A-line statement, 691 B-line statement, and 766 raw XML L count as distinct measurements.
openQuestions.question: How should DIMEV's 757-line description of A and Breul's historical endpoint f.28a be reconciled with Breul's statement that A contains 738 written lines and with the direct current boundary f.27v?

assertion.notes: Localization is inferential and must remain distinct from the c.1480 North-Midland copying context of witness A.

scope.description: Modern source-edition layers represented by the encoding

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme romance in which the demon-begotten Gowther grows into a violent outlaw, learns the truth of his birth, undertakes a divinely prescribed silent penance, defends an emperor's realm in disguise, restores a princess, and becomes associated with sanctity and miracles.

CME00115 preserves Breul's source title page and his section VI, Text, including the critical text and footnote apparatus; it does not encode the monograph's preface, introduction, grammar, metrical study, literary-historical investigation, separate annotations, glossary, bibliography, or appendices.

Breul's critical text has 756 editorial line positions in 63 twelve-line stanzas. His introduction says A contains 738 written lines and B 691; DIMEV v1.0 reports 757 for A. The raw XML has 766 L elements because ten lines of B's wholly divergent ending are embedded inside the apparatus note at critical line 754.

assertion.notes: Witness A has the running title 'sir gwother' and an explicit naming the work; witness B has no opening title and ends 'Explicit vita sancti'.

titles.supplied_by: Stable manuscript, editorial, and scholarly usage

assertion.notes: Orthography used in Breul's transcription and the encoded trailer 'Explicit Syr Goþer'.

scope.description: First line of Breul's represented critical text, supplied from witness B because witness A begins imperfectly

titles.value: God, that art of myghtis most

scope.description: A-version explicit retained after Breul's 756-position critical text

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Probably around 1400; surviving witnesses are later fifteenth-century copies; source edition 1886
work.regionDisplay: Probably the Northeast Midlands of England; witness A was copied in a North-Midland context
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme romance in which the demon-begotten Gowther grows into a violent outlaw, learns the truth of his birth, undertakes a divinely prescribed silent penance, defends an emperor's realm in disguise, restores a princess, and becomes associated with sanctity and miracles.
